questions.text: When the antinodes, in stationary waves, are passing through their equilibrium position, the energy is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: As an object undergoes SHM, such as a mass-spring system or a simple pendulum, its kinetic energy and potential energy continuously transform from one form to the other. At the equilibrium position, where the displacement is zero, the kinetic energy is maximized, as the object reaches its maximum velocity. Simultaneously, the potential energy is at its minimum, as the displacement from the equilibrium position is zero. As the object moves away from the equilibrium, the kinetic energy decreases, reaching zero at the extremum positions, where the potential energy is maximized. The potential energy stored in the system is then converted back into kinetic energy as the object returns towards the equilibrium position

Stationary waves do not cause any energy transfer but the particles present contain energy. Particles at antinode have maximum velocity and hence maximum kinetic energy while crossing the equilibrium position, while potential energy is zero so the energy is wholly kinetic. When the antinodes are all at their extreme displacements, the energy stored is wholly potential.

options.text: A combination of both forms of energy

questions.text: A traveling wave, in which the particles of the disturbed medium move perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The type of wave you're referring to is called a transverse wave. In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. This means that while the wave moves in one direction, the individual particles move up and down or side to side. Examples of transverse waves include electromagnetic waves (such as light and radio waves) and waves on a string or water surface.
In this wave motion, the particles travel along the direction of wave movement.
In a standing wave, energy does not flow.
A shock wave is a strong pressure wave in any elastic medium such as air, water, or a solid substance, produced by supersonic aircraft.

questions.explanation: Longitudinal waves are those waves in which particles of the disturbed medium move parallel to the direction of propagation of the wave. Transverse waves are those waves in which the particles of the disturbed medium move perpendicular to the direction of progagation of the wave. A shock wave is considered to be a longitudinal wave and a standing waves are not progressive or travelling waves as they do not transfer energy.

questions.text: If a wave can be polarized, it means, it is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a longitudinal wave, the oscillations occur parallel to the direction of wave propagation.
A stationary wave is formed by the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions with the same frequency and amplitude.
A superimposed wave refers to the combination of two or more waves that are added together to form a resultant wave.
Transverse wave, as polarization is a characteristic that can only be exhibited by transverse waves.

questions.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarised. Longitudinal waves cannot be polarised.Transverse: vibrations perpendicular to the direction of travel of the wave.Longitudinal: vibrations parallel to the direction of travel of the wave.Sound waves are longitudinal so they cannot be polarised.Polarised: the wave vibrations occur in one plane onlyUnpolarised: the wave vibrations occur in all planes perpendicular to the direction of travel.The reason that only transverse waves can be polarised is that their vibrations can potentially occur in all directions perpendicular to the direction of travel. It is therefore possible to confine the vibrations to a single plane. Or put another way an unpolarised transverse wave is a 3D shape, it is therefore possible to confine it to a 2D shape (a plane). The vibrations of a longitudinal wave occur along a single line, it is therefore not possible to confine that to a plane, i.e. a 1D shape cannot be confined within a 2D shape. Similarly, Stationary waves and superimposed waves can not be polarised as they are along a single line.

questions.explanation: To determine the apparent frequency heard by the listener, we use the Doppler effect formula:f' = f * (v + v_o) / (v + v_s)Here, f is the original frequency (1000 Hz), v is the speed of sound (340 m/s), v_o is the speed of the observer (0 m/s, as the observer is stationary), and v_s is the speed of the source (converted to 25 m/s from 90 km/h).Applying these values: f' = 1000 * (340 / (340 + 25)) = 931.5 Hz.The other options result from incorrect application of the Doppler effect formula, either due to incorrect speed conversions or misunderstanding the direction of motion, leading to incorrect frequency values.

questions.explanation: Laplace's correction to Newton’s formula recognizes that sound waves involve rapid compressions and rarefactions where there is insufficient time for heat exchange with surroundings, making the process adiabatic. Newton's original assumption of an isothermal process (constant temperature) was incorrect because it fails to consider the rapid temperature changes accompanying pressure variations in sound waves.The adiabatic process assumption accounts for these temperature changes, leading to a more accurate formula for sound speed in gases. The other processes—isochoric (constant volume) and isobaric (constant pressure)—do not describe the conditions experienced during sound wave propagation.

questions.text: The restoring force of SHM is maximum when particle:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The restoring force of Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) is maximum when the displacement of the particle is maximum. In SHM, the restoring force is given by Hooke's Law: F = -kx Where: F is the restoring force, k is the spring constant, and x is the displacement of the particle from its equilibrium position. The negative sign indicates that the restoring force acts in the opposite direction to the displacement. Now, let's consider the particle's displacement at different points in its motion. When the particle is at the maximum displacement (either positive or negative), the value of x is at its maximum. If we substitute the maximum displacement value into the equation, we get: F = -k * xmax Since the displacement is at its maximum, the magnitude of the restoring force is also at its maximum.

This statement is incorrect because the restoring force is actually zero when the particle is halfway between its maximum displacement and the equilibrium position. At this point, the particle is not experiencing any force that would cause it to return to the equilibrium position.

This statement is also incorrect. When the particle is crossing the mean position (equilibrium position), the restoring force is not at its maximum. In fact, the restoring force is zero at the mean position, as there is no displacement from the equilibrium position to generate a force.

This statement is incorrect as well. When the particle is at rest, it means that its velocity is zero. However, the restoring force is not at its maximum when the particle is at rest. The restoring force is maximum when the particle is at its maximum displacement, not when it is at rest.

questions.text: Which one of the following factors has no effect on the speed of sound in a gas?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Humidity refers to the amount of moisture or water vapor present in the air. It does have an effect on the speed of sound. Generally, as humidity increases, the speed of sound in a gas decreases. This is because water vapor molecules in humid air can slow down the transmission of sound waves.

Pressure is the force exerted by a gas per unit area. Interestingly, pressure does not have a direct effect on the speed of sound in a gas. Changes in pressure primarily affect the density of the gas, which indirectly influences the speed of sound.

Temperature plays a significant role in determining the speed of sound in a gas. As the temperature increases, the speed of sound also increases. This is because higher temperatures cause gas molecules to move faster, leading to faster sound wave propagation.

Density refers to the mass of a substance per unit volume. Density has a direct impact on the speed of sound in a gas. Generally, as the density of a gas increases, the speed of sound decreases. This is because denser gases have more molecules per unit volume, which can impede the transmission of sound waves.

questions.text: When a wave goes from one medium to another medium, which one of the following characteristics of the wave remains constant?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The velocity of a wave changes when it goes from one medium to another. The velocity of a wave depends on the properties of the medium through which it travels. When a wave enters a different medium, its velocity may increase or decrease, depending on the properties of the new medium.

The frequency of a wave remains constant as it moves from one medium to another. Frequency is the number of complete oscillations (cycles) of the wave that occur per unit time. It is a fundamental property of the wave and remains unchanged regardless of the medium it travels through.

The wavelength of a wave does not remain constant when it goes from one medium to another. Wavelength is the distance between two successive points in a wave that are in phase (e.g., crest to crest or trough to trough). It is determined by the source of the wave and remains the same as it moves through different media.

questions.explanation: The frequency of a wave remains constant as it moves from one medium to another. Frequency is the number of complete oscillations (cycles) of the wave that occur per unit time. questions.text: When a body is performing S.H.M then at its extreme position?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a body is performing Simple Harmonic Motion (S.H.M), its displacement refers to the distance from the equilibrium position (the central point in the motion) at any given time. At the extreme position, the displacement is zero because the body reaches its maximum distance from the equilibrium position and starts to reverse its direction.

Amplitude in S.H.M refers to the maximum distance the body moves away from the equilibrium position during one complete oscillation. At the extreme position, the amplitude is not zero; it is at its maximum value. If the amplitude were zero, the body would not undergo any motion, and it would stay at the equilibrium position.

At the extreme positions (the points where the body momentarily stops before changing direction) in S.H.M, the velocity of the body is zero. This is because the velocity changes sign at these points, going from positive to zero to negative (or vice versa), indicating that the body is momentarily at rest.

In Simple Harmonic Motion, the potential energy is zero at the extreme positions. Potential energy is at its maximum value at the equilibrium position and is converted into kinetic energy as the body moves away from the equilibrium position. At the extreme positions, all the potential energy is converted into kinetic energy, resulting in zero potential energy.

questions.text: The entropy of the universe always:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Option A (Decreases) is incorrect because it contradicts the second law of thermodynamics, which states that entropy tends to increase, not decrease.

The energy of the universe increases because energy never flows uphill spontaneously. Energy always flows downhill, and this causes an increase in entropy. Entropy is the spreading out of energy, and energy tends to spread out as much as possible. It flows spontaneously from a hot (i.e. highly energetic) region to a cold (less energetic) region. As a result, energy becomes evenly distributed across the two regions, and the temperature of the two regions becomes equal. The same thing happens on a much larger scale. The Sun and every other star are radiating energy into the universe. Mathematically, consider the total energy q that is transferred from a hot region at temperature T1 to a cold region at temperature T2. The entropy S1 of the hot region is defined as S1=qT1 The entropy S2 of the cold region is S2=qT2 Therefore, during the energy transfer, the change in entropy is ΔS=S2−S1 =qT2−qT1 =q(T2−T1) Since T2<T1,1T2>1T1​ and ΔS is positive. The total entropy of the system increases. Since energy never flows spontaneously in the other direction, the total entropy of the universe is always increasing.
Option C (Stays the same) is incorrect because it contradicts the second law of thermodynamics, which predicts that entropy tends to increase over time, not remain constant.

Option D (Both A and B) is incorrect because the entropy of the universe always increases according to the second law of thermodynamics. While there may be local decreases in entropy (such as in the formation of ordered structures), the overall trend is towards increasing entropy. Therefore, option B (Increases) is the correct choice.

questions.text: In which process the entire heat supplied to the gas is converted to the internal energy of the gas?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In an isochoric process, the volume of the gas remains constant (ΔV=0). Since there is no change in volume, no work is done by the gas (W=0). Therefore, any heat supplied to the gas solely increases its internal energy (ΔU=Q). As a result, in an isochoric process, the entire heat supplied to the gas is converted to the internal energy of the gas.

In an isobaric process, the pressure of the gas remains constant (ΔP=0). Work is done by the gas when its volume changes (W≠0). While some of the heat supplied may increase the internal energy of the gas, some of it may also be used to perform work on the surroundings. Therefore, in an isobaric process, not all the heat supplied to the gas is converted to its internal energy.

In an isothermal process, the temperature of the gas remains constant (ΔT=0). Work may be done by the gas when its volume changes (W≠0). While some of the heat supplied may increase the internal energy of the gas, some of it may also be used to perform work on the surroundings. Therefore, in an isothermal process, not all the heat supplied to the gas is converted to its internal energy.

In an adiabatic process, there is no heat exchange between the gas and its surroundings (Q=0). Work may be done by the gas when its volume changes (W≠0). While no heat is transferred, work done by the gas may change its internal energy. However, in this case, all the change in internal energy is due to the work done by the gas, as no heat is supplied. Therefore, in an adiabatic process, not all the heat supplied to the gas is converted to its internal energy because no heat is supplied.

questions.explanation: The formula for internal energy can be written as “ΔU=q+w” where we have q as the heat energy supplied to the system, w representing the work done on the system and ΔU is the change in the internal energy of the system. For all the heat energy to be converted to internal energy work done (w) should be 0. The formula for work done is W=PΔV, so for it to be zero there should be no change in volume (volume should be held constant), which is true for isochoric processes.

questions.text: Which of the following statement is not true about heat engine?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: All real engines are less efficient than Carnot engine
options.explanation: This statement is true. The Carnot engine is an ideal, reversible heat engine that operates between two heat reservoirs at different temperatures. It is the most efficient engine possible, and all real engines are less efficient than the Carnot engine due to various losses and inefficiencies.

options.text: All real engines are less efficient due to friction and heat losses
options.explanation: This statement is also true. Real engines experience various inefficiencies, such as friction in moving parts, heat losses to the surroundings, and incomplete combustion in internal combustion engines. These losses reduce the overall efficiency of real engines compared to the ideal Carnot engine.

options.text: Efficiency of Carnot engine working between same two temperatures, depends on the nature of working substance
options.explanation: The statement "Efficiency of the Carnot engine working between two same temperature, depending on the nature of the working substance" is not true about the heat engine.

options.text: The larger the temperature difference of two reservoirs, the greater is the efficiency.
options.explanation: This statement is true. The efficiency of any heat engine, including the Carnot engine, is higher when there is a larger temperature difference (Th - Tc) between the hot and cold reservoirs. A larger temperature difference allows more efficient conversion of heat energy into work, leading to higher efficiency.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct since it’s the only incorrect statement as the efficiency of a 'Carnot engine' depends solely on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs.The efficiency of a Carnot engine, which is the theoretical maximum efficiency for a heat engine operating between two temperature reservoirs, depends only on the temperatures of the reservoirs and is independent of the nature of the working substance. This efficiency is the maximum achievable efficiency for any heat engine operating between the same two temperature reservoirs. The Carnot efficiency is a function of the temperatures only and is not influenced by the specific properties of the working substance. The proof of this result is based on the second law of thermodynamics and relies on the assumption that the Carnot engine is reversible and operates in a cycle.In practice, no real engine can reach the Carnot efficiency because of irreversibilities and losses in actual processes. However, the Carnot efficiency serves as a benchmark for the maximum efficiency that any heat engine can achieve.
